text: The thoracic duct is visible in the surgical field, identifiable as a tubular structure within the thoracic cavity.
The surgeon uses a harmonic scalpel to control small branches from the descending aorta connecting to the esophagus. The left lung is visible in the surgical field.
The surgeon carefully freezes out the parabiotic tissue near the thoracic duct to avoid damaging it, with the thoracic duct visibly identifiable in the surgical field.
The surgeon identifies the left vagus nerve knob adjacent to the esophagus and proceeds to open the space between them before cutting the vagus nerve.
The surgeon identifies lymph nodes in the left hyaline region, noting their proximity to the vagus nerve. These lymph nodes are visually distinguished as pulmonary rather than esophageal lymph nodes.

The surgeon identifies the pulmonary vein and adjacent lymph nodes, noting an injury to the sinister pulmonary vein. The pulmonary ligament, numbered as a nine-year pulmonary ligament, is removed due to its shared lymph nodes between the lung and esophagus.
The surgeon operates in a straightforward area using an energy device, with no additional commentary provided on the specific actions or structures involved.
The surgeon navigates the area of diaphragmatic lymph nodes, moving upwards alongside the esophagus.
The surgeon cuts the right vagus nerve after dividing the pulmonary branch, using precise dissection techniques to separate these structures.
The surgeon observes an injury to the lung, indicating a need for future suturing. The surgeon examines a structure, possibly identifying it as a Nereidspazial node, labeled as a nine R lymph node.

The surgeon removes a diaphragmatic lymph node, identified as number fifteen, from a common area of metastasis near the rib. The lymph node is pulled up to expose the membrane portion of the diaphragm.
